latex: Farmer Bob's dairy farm is ready for milking day. His 199 cows line up in the barn and he begins milking them. The first cow gives 4 pints of milk. The next cow gives 5 pints of milk. The next gives 6 pints, and so on, increasing by 1 pint each cow. How many pints of milk does the last cow give?
notes: }  \large  \textbf{Answer:} Compared to the first cow, the second cow gives one pint more, or $4+1$ pints. The third cow gives 1 pint more than the second cow, so it gives $(4+1)+1$ pints. Because addition is associative (parentheses don't matter), we can eliminate the parentheses and still come up with the same result. Thus, the third cow yields $4+1+1$ pints. The fourth gives $4+1+1+1$ pints, the fifth gives $4+1+1+1+1$ pints, and so on. Each time we move on to the next cow, the yield increases by 1. Thus, for the 199th cow, its yield increases 198 times from the first cow. Thus, we are trying to find\begin{align*} 4+\underbrace{(1+1+1+\cdots+1)}_{\text{198 ones}}. \end{align*}The 198 1's add to 198, so we have\begin{align*} 4+\underbrace{(1+1+1+\cdots+1)}_{\text{198 ones}} &= 4+(198) \\ &=\boxed{202}. \end{align*}  }
